name: River Park of Arga – Pamplona/Iruña
region_intro: Hiking around Pampelune offers a diverse range of landscapes, from urban green spaces to mountainous foothills. The city is situated in the Basin of Pamplona, a transition zone between the Atlantic façade and the drier Iberian Peninsula, contributing to varied terrain. The Arga River flows through the city, providing extensive fluvial parks with walking paths, while the proximity to the Pyrenees offers access to more challenging mountain trails. Additionally, the region features unique semi-desert landscapes like Bardenas Reales and dense forests such as Irati Forest within reach. Best hiking trails around Pampelune The most popular hiking route is City tour of Pamplona/Iruña – circular via La Ciudadela and Parque Fluvial del Arga, a 6.1 miles (9.9 km) trail that takes 2 hours 35 minutes to complete. This route combines urban landmarks with serene riverside paths along the Arga River. Another top favourite among local hikers is Ezkaba - Fuerte de San Cristobal via GR 220 trail, a moderate 6.4 miles (10.4 km) path. This trail ascends to Fuerte de San Cristobal, offering panoramic views of the surrounding area. Local hikers also love the River Park of Arga – Pamplona/Iruña, a 9.8 miles (15.8 km) trail leading through the extensive fluvial park, often completed in about 4 hours 7 minutes. Hiking around Pampelune is defined by urban green spaces, extensive riverside paths, and mountain foothills. The network offers options for different ability levels, from leisurely strolls to more challenging ascents. The routes in Pampelune are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.2 stars from more than 400 reviews. More than 1900 hikers have used komoot to explore Pampelune's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Pamplona?
A: There are over 202 hiking routes around Pamplona, offering a wide variety of experiences from urban strolls to more challenging ascents in the foothills. The komoot community has explored these trails over 1900 times.

Q: Are there family-friendly hiking options in Pamplona?
A: Yes, Pamplona offers many family-friendly hiking options. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e968871462/river-park-of-arga-pamplona-iruna" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;River Park of Arga – Pamplona/Iruña&lt;/a&gt; is an excellent choice, providing extensive, well-maintained paths along the river that are suitable for all ages. The city's green spaces and sections of the Arga River Park are generally flat and accessible.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on hikes near Pamplona?
A: The terrain around Pamplona is quite varied. Within the city, you'll find flat, paved paths along the Arga River and through urban parks. Moving outwards, the Basin of Pamplona offers gentle rolling hills, while areas closer to the Pyrenees, like the Ezkaba mountain, feature steeper ascents and more rugged trails. You can also find unique semi-desert landscapes like Bardenas Reales within reach for a different experience.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes around Pamplona?
A: Yes, many routes around Pamplona are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point. A popular example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e968894545/city-tour-of-pamplona-iruna-circular-via-la-ciudadela-and-parque-fluvial-del-arga" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;City tour of Pamplona/Iruña – circular via La Ciudadela and Parque Fluvial del Arga&lt;/a&gt;, which combines urban exploration with riverside paths.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or viewpoints I can see on hikes?
A: Hiking around Pamplona offers several interesting landmarks and panoramic viewpoints. You can explore the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4396525" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pamplona Cathedral and the Citadel&lt;/a&gt;, or walk along the ancient city walls. For stunning views, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e966250324/ezkaba-fuerte-de-san-cristobal-via-gr-220-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ezkaba - Fuerte de San Cristobal via GR 220 trail&lt;/a&gt;, which ascends to the Fuerte de San Cristobal, offering expansive vistas of the surrounding area. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/654847" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pilgrim Monument at Alto del Perdón&lt;/a&gt; is another significant landmark on a more challenging route.

Q: Are there challenging hikes for experienced hikers?
A: Yes, for those seeking a challenge, Pamplona offers routes with significant elevation gain. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10516606" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto del Perdón Memorial Cross – St. Andrew's Church loop from Pamplona&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 18 km hike with over 300 meters of elevation gain, requiring good fitness. The proximity to the Pyrenees also means more demanding mountain trails are accessible for day trips.

Q: What is the best time of year for hiking in Pamplona?
A: The best time for hiking in Pamplona is generally during spring (April-June) and autumn (September-October) when the weather is mild and pleasant. Summers can be hot, especially in the drier inland areas, while winters can bring cooler temperatures and occasional rain, though urban and lower-elevation trails remain accessible.

Q: Can I reach hiking trails using public transport from Pamplona?
A: Many urban and peri-urban trails, particularly those along the Arga River and within the city's green belt, are easily accessible by public transport or even on foot from central Pamplona. For trails further afield, local bus services might connect to nearby towns, but checking specific routes and schedules in advance is recommended.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in Pamplona?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 400 reviews. Reviewers often praise the diverse landscapes, from the serene riverside paths of the Arga River Park to the historic city walls and the rewarding views from higher points like Fuerte de San Cristobal. The blend of natural beauty and cultural heritage is frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there any long-distance walking routes that pass through Pamplona?
A: Yes, Pamplona is a significant point on the &lt;a href="https://www.visitnavarra.es/en/hiking" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino de Santiago&lt;/a&gt;, specifically the Camino Francés. Pilgrims often enter the city via the Magdalena Bridge. This offers opportunities for longer walks with historical significance, either as part of the pilgrimage or for shorter sections.

Q: Where can I find parking for hiking trailheads near Pamplona?
A: For urban hikes, public parking garages are available throughout Pamplona. For trails slightly outside the city, such as those leading to Ezkaba or along the Arga River, designated parking areas are often available near trailheads or in nearby villages. It's advisable to check specific route details on komoot for parking information relevant to your chosen starting point.

Q: Are there any hikes that explore the historic city walls of Pamplona?
A: Yes, Pamplona's 16th-century defensive walls, a National Monument, offer scenic walks. A route like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20515857" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pamplona City Walls – Pamplona Cathedral and the Citadel loop from Pamplona / Iruña&lt;/a&gt; provides an excellent opportunity to explore these historic fortifications, combining cultural interest with a pleasant walk.
tips: Over the bed of the Ultzama River is this picturesque bridge that dates back to the 13th century and was built with the purpose of crossing the river, joining the old Roman Road and later the Camino de Santiago.

This is the most important bridge of the four medieval ones that cross the Arga as it passes through Pamplona.

It was built in the twelfth century and later renovated in the nineteenth century. It consists of five large slightly pointed arches, triangular tajamares and semicircular discharge arches on the supports. It is related to that of Puente la Reina. A transept, probably built in the sixteenth century, points the traveler at one end of the bridge to the entrance of the Camino de Santiago to the city. In it you can see the image of the Jacobean saint.

After crossing this Romanesque bridge, the pilgrim arrives at the Tejería Park and from there to the walls of Pamplona, under the old city of Navarrería, which is accessed by the Portal de Francia.
https://www.pamplona.es/turismo/puente-de-la-magdalena#:~:text=Este%20es%20el%20puente%20m%C3%A1s,medio%20punto%20en%20los%20apoyos.
